summary: DeVault shares his approach to long-term phone maintenance, arguing against the cycle of annual upgrades. He advocates for installing custom ROMs like LineageOS to remove bloatware, insisting on user-replaceable batteries, using protective cases, and attempting repairs before buying new devices. Using his Samsung Galaxy S5 as an example, he demonstrates that phones can remain fast and functional for years with proper care. The post frames planned obsolescence as a consumer trap that can be resisted through technical self-sufficiency.
key_points: Stock ROMs are laden with hundreds of background apps and services that spy on users, drain batteries, and slow phones down as a form of planned obsolescence
Non-replaceable batteries are an obvious planned obsolescence tactic; a $10 battery replacement extends a phone's life by years
DIY repairs are more accessible than people think — a $6 USB daughterboard and an hour of work saved buying a new phone
Installing LineageOS or Replicant without Google apps keeps a phone running as fast as the day you bought it
money_quotes: I don't want to be buying a new phone every year. That's a lot of money!
There were literally hundreds of apps and services with scary permissions running in the background that could not be removed.
These spy on you, wear down your battery, and slow down your phone over time - another form of planned obsolescence.
My phone is still as fast as the day I got it.
Non-user-replacable batteries are an obvious form of planned obsolescence.
Batteries don't last forever and you should never buy a phone that you cannot replace the battery of.
The whole process was a lot easier than I thought it would be.
Don't let consumerism get the better of you!
key_insight: With custom software, replaceable parts, and a willingness to do basic repairs, a phone can last for years — resisting the planned obsolescence that manufacturers rely on.
